items.heading: Unlocking SPIKE Prime with 3D Printing
items.summary: SPIKE Prime transforms from a robotics set to a real engineering platform when students use Tinkercad and 3D printing to create custom parts. This allows for expanded movement and personalized designs.
items.text: 👉 Have we already explored 100% of SPIKE Prime’s potential?  Because once students start designing their own robot parts in Tinkercad…  SPIKE Prime suddenly stops being just a robotics set.  And starts becoming a platform for real engineering.  ⚙️ Custom mechanisms 🖨️ 3D-printed rack gears 📏 Larger movement ranges 🎨 Personalized robot elements  For example:  A standard SPIKE Prime plotter has a limited drawing range.  But when students design and print longer rack gears themselves, the robot suddenly becomes capable of drawing across an entire A4 page.  Not because the hardware changed.  But because students expanded the system themselves.  🧠 This is the moment when students begin to understand something important:  Technology is not a closed box.  It is something they can redesign, extend and improve.  And this is where 3D printing becomes much more than just “printing gadgets”.  #RoboCamp #LEGOEducation #EducationalRobotics #STEMEducation #EdTech #CodingForKids #SPIKEPrime #3DPrinting #Tinkercad #3DDesign

items.heading: Short 45-Minute Robotics Lessons: Adapting to School Schedules
items.summary: The new LEGO Education Computer Science & AI set supports 45-minute lessons, emphasizing the importance of a well-structured lesson plan. Charging logistics are crucial due to multiple wireless components.
items.text: Testing the new LEGO Education Computer Science & AI set Part #1: 45-minute lessons  45-minute robotics lessons are not a new idea.  But they are becoming more and more important in real school timetables.  LEGO Education is now clearly positioning the new Computer Science & AI set around short, 45-minute classroom lessons — and honestly, we think this is the right direction.  ⏱️ Teachers need robotics activities that can fit into a regular school day.  But here is the key point:  A short lesson is not created by the box.  It is created by the lesson plan.  That is why we have been developing short SPIKE Prime lessons for some time already.  For example, in our SPIKE Prime Forklift lesson, students work with mechanics, movement logic and structured programming — all within a compact classroom format.  Now we are testing a similar idea with the new LEGO Education Computer Science & AI set.  Can you run a 45-minute lesson with the new set?  Yes.  But can you run several 45-minute lessons smoothly, one after another, during a normal school day?  That is a different question.  🔋 With SPIKE Prime, this part of the classroom workflow was simpler: one hub, one battery, one charging routine.  With the new Computer Science & AI electronics, each wireless component has its own battery. This opens up some interesting design possibilities, but it also means charging has to be planned before the lesson — it is not something teachers can always easily fix once the lesson has started.  So this is not really a question of whether the new set is “bad”.  It is not.  It is a question of what schools actually need.  If your goal is to run better 45-minute robotics lessons, you do not automatically need a new set.  You need a better lesson plan.  #RoboCamp #LEGOEducation #EducationalRobotics #STEMEducation #EdTech #CodingForKids #SPIKEPrime #ComputerScience #AIinEducation #HandsOnLearning

items.heading: SPIKE Essential: Advanced Problem-Solving in Primary School
items.summary: Students use SPIKE Essential to create a robot that navigates a maze, demonstrating advanced computational thinking skills. Lesson structure is key to supporting complex problem-solving.
items.text: A primary school classroom. Students working with SPIKE Essential.  And a task that is clearly not “basic”.  ⚙️ Two motors. 📏 Precise movement. 🤖 A robot navigating a maze.  This is not the kind of problem you would typically expect from this set.  And yet — it works.  Not because of the hardware. But because of how the lesson is designed.  When the structure is right, even simple tools can support much more advanced thinking: – breaking problems into steps, – testing and improving, – understanding how code translates into movement.  🧠 This is what real computational thinking looks like — even in younger grades.  #RoboCamp #LEGOEducation #EducationalRobotics #STEMEducation #EdTech #ComputationalThinking #SPIKEEssential

items.heading: Innovative SPIKE Prime Beginner Lessons
items.summary: SPIKE Prime Beginner course focuses on clear structure, computational thinking, and creative exploration. Students implement random variables and structured programming to foster innovation.
items.text: Short robotics lessons don’t have to be a limitation. They can be a teaching tool.  When the core of an exercise is clearly structured, students don’t spend the entire lesson just trying to “finish the task.” They gain time for something more important — experimentation.  🎲 Game Dice Students learn how to implement a random variable (1–6) and apply it in their own board game.  🎵 Music Box Structured programming first — creative composition next.  🏗️ Forklift Mechanics, movement logic and then custom challenges.  This is what our SPIKE Prime Beginner course is built around:  Clear structure. Real computational thinking. Space for creative exploration.  Want to see the full course outline? 👉 Check the first link in the comments.  #RoboCamp #LEGOEducation #SPIKEPrime #RoboticsEducation #ComputationalThinking #STEMeducation #EdTech #CodingInSchool

items.heading: Empowering Creativity Through Structured Lessons
items.summary: The 'Music Box' lesson from RoboCamp encourages students to go beyond basics, adding their own compositions. A well-designed lesson plan fosters creative experimentation.
items.text: A short lesson scenario doesn’t have to be a limitation. It can be a powerful teaching tool.  When the core of an exercise is well designed and built around a clear structure, students don’t spend the entire lesson just trying to “finish the task.” They gain time for something more important — experimentation.  In this implementation of the “Music Box” lesson from RoboCamp, students didn’t stop at the basic version. They added their own musical compositions and modified the build.  This is the moment when a lesson plan stops being an instruction sheet and becomes a starting point for creative thinking.  Great work by the team at Beyond Blocks. Congratulations!  #robocamp #LEGOEducation #SPIKEprime #STEM #STEMEducation #stemforkids #edtech

items.heading: Teaching AI with Existing SPIKE Prime Resources
items.summary: The Launcher AI+ activity with SPIKE Prime teaches students about AI learning through iteration and feedback, fostering understanding without relying on new hardware.
items.text: The recent announcement of LEGO Education’s Computer Science & AI sets raises an important question: should AI in robotics education focus on using AI tools, or on understanding how learning algorithms actually work?  Understanding how AI learns — through feedback, adaptation, and iteration — can already be taught today, even with robotics sets that many schools already have in their classrooms.  One example is Launcher AI+, an advanced SPIKE Prime activity developed by the RoboCamp team.  Students build a catapult-style robot that repeatedly launches a ball toward a target. After each attempt, the robot receives feedback — the shot was too short or too far — and adjusts its launch power accordingly. With every iteration, the program updates its parameters and gradually improves accuracy.  There is no black-box AI here. Students design the learning logic themselves and can clearly see how data and feedback shape the robot’s behavior over time.  This is what learning about AI can look like — without new hardware, and without using AI as a shortcut instead of understanding how it works.  If you’d like to learn more about this approach, you’ll find additional details in the comments below.  #robocamp #LEGOEducation #SPIKEprime #STEM #STEMEducation #stemforkids #edtech #AIinEducation #machinelearningeducation

items.heading: Upcoming Retirement of SPIKE Sets and New AI Line
items.summary: SPIKE Prime and Essential will be available until mid-2026, with support until 2031. The new Computer Science & AI line awaits evaluation.
items.text: LEGO® Education has announced a new Computer Science & AI product line and, at the same time, shared an update regarding the retirement of SPIKE Prime and SPIKE Essential.  For schools already working with SPIKE sets, the following confirmed timelines are particularly important: – SPIKE Prime and SPIKE Essential will remain available for purchase at least until mid-2026, – spare parts for SPIKE Prime and SPIKE Essential will be available until June 2028, – software for SPIKE will remain supported until June 2031.  This means that the well-established SPIKE Prime and SPIKE Essential sets will continue to be reliable, long-term educational tools for many years to come.  As for the new Computer Science & AI sets — at this stage, it is simply too early to evaluate them. We don’t form opinions until we have had the opportunity to work hands-on with the sets and test them in real classroom conditions.  We will publish a full review of the new sets after proper testing.  In the meantime, we invite you to read our review of LEGO Education Science. Since the new Computer Science & AI line is based on the same generation of electronic components, it is currently the best available reference point for understanding the direction LEGO Education is heading with its future educational products.  Link to the review in the comments.  #robocamp #LEGOEducation #SPIKEprime #SPIKEEssential #educationalrobotics #STEM #stemeducation #stemforkids #edtech

items.heading: From Basic Mechanism to Applied Programming with SPIKE Prime
items.summary: In a Grades IV-V classroom, SPIKE Prime transforms basic mechanisms into lessons on real programming. A clear structure enables progression from building to critical thinking.
items.text: A primary school classroom. Grades IV–V. SPIKE Prime in use.  A simple mechanism: – forward / backward movement 🔁 – motor control ⚙️ – repeatable motion 🎯  At first glance — basic.  But this is already real programming. Not just building.  Students are not stopping at assembling the model. They are controlling it. Testing it. Adjusting it.  And this is where the difference appears.  When the lesson has a clear structure, students move faster from “building” to actual thinking: – cause → effect 🧠 – code → movement 🤖 – testing → improvement 🔍  This is what happens when robotics is treated as a system — not just a set.  The hardware stays the same. The outcomes don’t.  📘 This is lesson 1 out of 100 in our SPIKE Prime curriculum. The starting point is simple — but it’s designed as part of a long-term progression.  Implemented in a real school setting — as part of a funded education project.  #RoboCamp #LEGOEducation #EducationalRobotics #STEMEducation #ComputationalThinking #CodingInSchool #SPIKEPrime

items.heading: Random Variables and Game Design with SPIKE Prime
items.summary: Students use SPIKE Prime to create a random number generator, designing board games to apply this knowledge, promoting creativity and experimentation.
items.text:
A simple robot can teach a random variable.

Students build a basic random number generator (1–6).
Then they design their own board game around it.

The build is simple.
The thinking is not.

When the structure is clear, there is space for experimentation.

That’s where creativity begins.

#RoboCamp #LEGOEducation #SPIKEPrime #ComputationalThinking #RoboticsEducation

items.heading: Flying Bird Project: Crank Mechanisms and Design Skills
items.summary: The Flying Bird project uses SPIKE Prime and mBot 2 sets, coupled with laser cutting, to teach crank mechanisms and enhance design skills.
items.text:
🦉 Flying Bird – SPIKE Prime / mBot 2 / laser cutter

Build a moving owl model with your students through the Flying Bird project. Wooden elements and string create a crank mechanism powered by a motor, illustrating mechanical principles in practice.
• Construction of a working mechanism
• Understanding the principle of a crank
• Development of design skills

The model can be constructed using the LEGO SPIKE Prime or mBot 2 set, along with elements cut with a laser cutter, allowing for the creation of complex mechanisms.

items.heading: Measuring Wind with SPIKE Prime and 3D Printing
items.summary: Build a functional anemometer with SPIKE Prime using a 3D-printed propeller from Tinkercad, enabling measurement of wind speed in real time.
items.text:
🌬️ What is wind — and how can we measure its strength with LEGO®?

In this lesson plan, students build a fully functional anemometer using LEGO® Education SPIKE™ Prime and a custom 3D-printed propeller. It’s a perfect way to combine STEM learning, engineering and real-world data collection — all in one engaging class. 📊💡

Here’s what the lesson includes:
1️⃣ A propeller designed from scratch in Autodesk Tinkercad,
2️⃣ Step-by-step building and programming instructions,
3️⃣ As a final result: a LEGO robot that measures wind speed in real time! 🌬️

🎯 To complete the lesson, you’ll need:
🔹 Autodesk Tinkercad
🔹 A 3D printer
🔹 LEGO® Education SPIKE™ Prime set

Looking for new ways to bring innovation into your classroom? This one is definitely worth a try! 🚀

#3DPrintingInEducation #LEGOEducation #SPIKEPrime #EducationalRobotics #STEMEducation #3DPrintYourRobot #MakerEducation #LessonPlanning #InnovativeTeaching #PythonForKids

items.heading: Efficient Workflow with 45-Minute AI Lessons
items.summary: The Computer Science & AI set is tested for compatibility with 45-minute lessons, emphasizing the need for efficient lesson plans rather than new sets.
items.text:
Testing the new LEGO Education Computer Science & AI set
Part #1: 45-minute lessons

⏱️ 45-minute robotics lessons are not created by the box.

They are created by the lesson plan.

That is why we have been developing short SPIKE Prime lessons for some time already — including our Forklift lesson, designed for a compact classroom format.

Now we are testing a similar format with the new Computer Science & AI set.

Can it work in 45 minutes?

Yes.

But can it work smoothly lesson after lesson, during a normal school day?

That is where workflow starts to matter.

🔋 With SPIKE Prime, charging was simple: one hub, one battery, one routine.

With the new wireless electronics, charging has to be planned before the lesson — not fixed once the class has already started.

So if your goal is to run better 45-minute robotics lessons, you do not automatically need a new set.

You need a better lesson plan.

#RoboCamp #LEGOEducation #EducationalRobotics
#STEMEducation #EdTech #CodingForKids
#SPIKEPrime #ComputerScience #AIinEducation #HandsOnLearning

items.heading: Centrifugal Force Demonstrated with Carousel Model

items.text:

Demonstrate centrifugal force to your students with the Carousel model. The faster it spins, the more the airplanes tilt away from the vertical position, allowing observation of physics in action.

• Development of analytical skills

This robot construction can be created using the LEGO SPIKE Prime or mBot 2 set, along with precisely cut elements using a laser cutter, ensuring accuracy and aesthetic quality.
